policy_statement: Build capacity of government and non-government stakeholders for deployment of coastal adaptation activities.
mechanism_description: Building the skills and capabilities of public and private stakeholders enables more effective planning and implementation of coastal climate adaptation actions.
typology_evidence_quote: Capacity-building for government and non- government stakeholders on the deployment of adaptation activities.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building → ↑ institutional adaptive capacity → better adaptation planning/implementation → ↓ climate vulnerability and exposure
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes the capabilities of public agencies, private actors, and other stakeholders through training, technical assistance, coordination, knowledge development, and governance strengthening. (2) The mediating process is improved institutional learning, decision quality, preparedness, coordination, and implementation capacity, which enables more effective design and execution of coastal adaptation measures. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced vulnerability, exposure, and disruption from coastal climate hazards such as sea-level rise, storm surge, coastal flooding, and erosion.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is to increase institutional and stakeholder capacity to plan and carry out actions that reduce vulnerability and exposure to coastal climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism works by strengthening stakeholder and institutional capability, which improves adaptation planning and implementation and thereby reduces vulnerability to climate hazards.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: It is not primarily Mitigation because the mechanism does not directly reduce greenhouse gas emissions; not Resource Efficiency because efficiency gains are not the main climate effect; and not Nature-Based Solutions because any ecosystem measures that may later be implemented are downstream possibilities, not the mechanism itself.  
3. Edge cases applied: Capacity-building policies can be cross-cutting and may indirectly support mitigation or nature-based actions, but under the dominant-pathway rule the classification follows the main immediate climate effect shared by all such policies: stronger adaptive capacity.  
4. Confirm the primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses this mechanism—building stakeholder skills and institutional capability for adaptation—has adaptation capacity as its core climate-relevant effect, regardless of the specific coastal context or later project choices.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: whether a given policy supports coastal flooding, sea-level rise, governance reform, or implementation of specific adaptation measures should be assigned only with policy-specific evidence, not at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: This is a governance/enabling mechanism rather than a direct physical adaptation measure, but it still classifies as Adaptation because its principal purpose and effect are to reduce climate risk through enhanced adaptive capacity. It could indirectly enable mitigation or nature-based interventions, but those are secondary and contingent.
key_indicators: “Capacity-building”; “government and non-government stakeholders”; “deployment of adaptation activities”; “adaptation activities”
co_benefits: Improved institutional coordination, governance capacity, stakeholder engagement, and general disaster preparedness.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also resemble an incentive-program if delivered through grants or funded training, but the text only specifies capacity-building and not a financial transfer mechanism. “Coastal” appears in the policy statement but not verbatim text; however, the assigned causal detail and location context support interpreting the adaptation focus as coastal and climate-related.
